q3: The model kept landing the individual pieces but struggled to hold the whole voice together at once — it would nail the body copy and then the headline would fall flat, or vice versa. It wasn't wrong, it just took a few passes to get everything in the same register simultaneously.
q5: I looked at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about — tonal calibration and the 'one beat that sounds like a person said it' criterion is basically what I was chasing the whole session. Where it fell short is that it's still framed around the product's job, not the user's experience of reading it. The rubric would pass copy that's technically correct but still feels assembled rather than written.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice the model was consistently stronger on 'economy and precision' than on 'contextual presence' — the copy was tight but it sometimes felt like it could have lived on any empty state screen, not specifically this one. Without the rubric I probably would have just said the voice felt slightly off without being able to pinpoint why.

q3: The model kept confusing 'outcome-oriented' with 'hand-holdy' — 'Save Your First Payee' is not the same thing as a label that tells you what clicking achieves. And then it abandoned a locked, working headline and body entirely, which was the most disorienting mistake of the whole session.
q5: I looked at the rubric, and it actually captured a lot of what I care about — the outcome-oriented CTA principle is basically my button label philosophy, and the tonal calibration criteria maps closely to how I think about context-shifting. But the rubric didn't stop the model from making the worst mistake of the session, which was nuking the locked copy. A rubric only helps if the model is actually reading what the user has explicitly protected.
q6: Yes — the rubric made it obvious that the model was applying criteria in isolation rather than as a system. It would optimize for 'user-centered direction' on the button label without holding onto 'contextual presence' for the full unit. Seeing the rubric spelled out made me realize the model was treating each criterion as a separate checklist item rather than understanding that all five have to coexist on the same screen at the same time.

q3: The model kept defaulting to reassurance — 'you're set from there,' 'goes out on time' — when I was asking for something more declarative and mechanical. It understood the register problem intellectually but kept solving it with warmth instead of precision. The tonal seam between headline and subtext was the core issue and it never fully resolved it.
q5: I did look at it, and honestly it captured a lot of what I care about — especially the tonal calibration criterion and the part about CTAs describing outcomes not actions. But seeing it made me realize the model was sometimes using the rubric as cover for changes I hadn't asked for, like flipping 'Start Sending Payments' back to 'Set Up Payments' and then attributing it to rubric guidance. That's not useful — it's the model outsourcing a bad call to a framework.
q6: Yes, actually. The rubric made visible something I might have chalked up to 'the draft just isn't landing' — the model was applying tonal warmth uniformly instead of letting different elements do different emotional jobs. Once I saw 'tonal calibration' named explicitly as a criterion, I realized the model understood the concept in the abstract but kept collapsing it in practice. Every draft came out at roughly the same temperature.
